text: Robotic abdominal perineal resection, APR, is one of the latest minimally invasive techniques for removal of low rectal cancer. This technique is used when the tumor invades the anal sphincter complex, the oncologic APR, or when the patient's anal function will not tolerate a low anterior resection with coloanal anastomosis, a functional APR. These two different indications for APR also induce some variation in the perineal approach that we believe should be tailored. The robotic platform offers a stable vision and added precision for surgeons, making it possible to progress further the abdominal dissection in order to fulfill the required steps for each tailored perineal approach.

There is a need for standardization of the procedure to make it reproducible and oncologically safe. We present our video showing the steps we believe should be taken as standard to perform robotic APR.

The surgeon creates pneumoperitoneum through an umbilical port using a vertical incision, which is closed with a J-suture. Four robotic ports, each eight millimeters in diameter, are placed in an oblique line starting from two to three centimeters superior and medial to the right anterior superior iliac spine and ending in the left upper quadrant. The ports are spaced six to eight centimeters apart, adjusted for the patient's BMI and abdominal cavity width.

An assistant port measuring twelve millimeters is placed parallel and eight centimeters superior to the robotic ports line. This port facilitates the placement of locking clips, swabs, and the introduction of a standard laparoscopic linear stapler. An additional five millimeter middle port may be placed to enhance retraction in patients with increased BMI.

The patient is positioned in Trendelenburg and right side tilt to enhance exposure. The greater omentum is placed cranially under the liver, and small bowel loops are gently pushed to the right upper quadrant. The root of the small bowel mesentery, duodenal jejunal flexure, and inferior mesenteric vein are visible, with a swab marking the area between the duodenal jejunal flexure and IMV. A retraction stitch is applied to the bladder's peritoneum in males or uterus in females for better visualization of the peritoneal reflection.

The robotic arms are docked with the following arrangement: Robotic arm R-I holds a fenestrated bipolar grasper, R-II is equipped with a camera, R-III carries monopolar scissors, and R-IV holds a grasper for macro retraction using a cadier or tip-up forceps.

The assistant retracts the sigmoid colon laterally while the robotic arm holds and retracts the sigmoid mesocolon, revealing the mesocolic pedicle. Using monopolar diathermic scissors, the surgeon scores the sigmoid mesocolon medial to the right ureter, defining the embryological planes between the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) and hypogastric nerves. The IMA pedicle is dissected away from the nerves on the right pelvic sidewall, and the dissection continues proximally towards the artery's origin. The IMA is ligated and divided one centimeter distal to the aorta to prevent nerve damage.

The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection until the colon is visible. Cranially, the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) is identified, and the mesocolon is scored below it to find the planes between the IMV and the retroperitoneal fascia. The dissection proceeds laterally to connect the dissection planes. The IMV origin near the duodenal jejunal flexure is divided after secure ligation with locking clips. The mobilization is completed by dissecting the left paracolic gutter from lateral to medial.

The surgeon enters the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane and begins dissection behind the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA). The posterior plane guides lateral dissection, with nerves carefully dissected away from the mesorectum on each side. An anterior pretonal incision is made between the mesorectum and vagina in females, or prostate and seminal vesicles in males, opening the plane caudally and laterally to connect with the previously dissected TME plane behind the mesorectum.

The surgeon continues dissection in the timid plane around the mesorectum, progressing towards the pelvic floor. Tailored dissection of the pelvic floor muscles is performed based on the type of abdominoperineal resection (APR) indication. For rectal cancer involving the anal sphincter complex, an extra-elevator approach is used on the affected side. The anterior limit of abdominal dissection is at the level of the seminal vesicles in males and the rectal vaginal septum in females, while the posterior limit is at the tip of the coccyx and the levator muscles.

The surgeon performs an intersynthetic approach for functional APR, extending dissection through the pelvic floor inside the levator muscles. The sigmoid colon is disconnected from the rectum using a linear stapler, and a colostomy is created using the distal end of the divided descending colon.

The patient is positioned in a prone jackknife position for perineal dissection. A longitudinal incision is made over the coccyx and extended to an elliptical incision encompassing the anal verge. The incision is deepened from the skin to the coccyx, around the external anal sphincters. The coccyx is disconnected from the sacrum, and the pelvic floor muscles are excised based on the lateral tumor involvement.

The surgical specimen is removed through the perineal approach. The pelvic floor defect is repaired using the greater omentum, and drainage is placed through the abdominal cavity. A biological or composite mesh is used to reinforce the repair, fixed in a hammock-like fashion to minimize tension.
The surgeon closes the perineal wound in layers, leaving the anterior part of the wound open with an iodo-povidone soaked dressing placed inside for perineal drainage.
